section_title: General rider emergency in vehicle
section_text: If a rider reports an emergency while in the vehicle: 1) Stay calm and keep the rider on the line. 2) Ask the rider to briefly describe the emergency (medical, safety, external threat, etc.). 3) Confirm the rider's current condition (conscious/unconscious, able to speak, any immediate danger). 4) Confirm the vehicle's status (moving, stopped, pulled over). 5) If there is any immediate risk to life or serious injury, contact local emergency services according to the Emergency Contact SOP and provide vehicle location. 6) Notify the on-duty safety lead or supervisor. 7) Document the incident in the incident tracking system with as much detail as possible after the rider is safe.

section_title: Medical emergency in vehicle
section_text: If a rider reports a medical emergency: 1) Ask if the rider can safely exit the vehicle at the next safe stop. 2) If they cannot exit safely or are in severe distress, instruct the vehicle (through standard procedures) to pull over at the nearest safe location if it is not already stopped. 3) Immediately contact local emergency services, provide the precise vehicle location, and follow their instructions. 4) Keep the rider on the line if possible, provide reassurance, and avoid giving medical advice beyond reading approved guidance if applicable. 5) Notify the safety lead and log the incident as 'Medical - High Priority'. 6) After emergency services take over, arrange an alternative vehicle for other riders if needed and capture a brief summary of actions taken. Escalate if: The rider becomes unresponsive, or the situation involves a minor (under 18).

section_title: Police interaction involving a vehicle
section_text: When law enforcement interacts with a vehicle or requests information: 1) Confirm whether the vehicle is currently stopped and that riders are safe. 2) If speaking with an officer via phone, identify yourself as remote support and follow the Law Enforcement Interaction Guidelines document. 3) If the officer is on scene, use any approved external communication methods to inform them that the vehicle is a driverless test or commercial vehicle, as documented in the guidelines. 4) Avoid sharing rider personal data beyond what is allowed by policy and law; direct the officer to the designated legal or safety contact if they request more information. 5) Notify the safety lead and log the interaction with time, location, badge number if available, and a short description. Escalate if: The officer requests to impound the vehicle, or issues a citation.

section_title: Vehicle blocked by obstacle (pylon, cone, stalled car)
section_text: If a vehicle is stopped and appears blocked by an obstacle (e.g., construction cone, pylon, stalled vehicle) and cannot proceed autonomously: 1) Verify via available cameras and telemetry that it is safe to remain stopped. 2) Check traffic conditions around the vehicle to ensure riders are not at immediate risk. 3) If riders are on board, explain that the vehicle has paused due to an obstacle and that you are working to resolve it. 4) Consult the remote guidance or tele-assistance tool (if available) to determine if a safe alternative path around the obstacle can be taken according to policy. 5) If remote guidance can safely navigate around the obstacle, follow the procedure to authorize that manoeuvre. 6) If the obstacle cannot be bypassed safely, arrange an alternative vehicle for the rider and keep the original vehicle stopped until the obstacle is cleared or a ground team intervenes. 7) Log the obstacle type, location, and resolution for future analysis.

section_title: Vehicle stuck at complex intersection
section_text: If a vehicle is repeatedly pausing or not progressing at a complex intersection: 1) Confirm via telemetry that there is no immediate safety hazard to riders or other road users. 2) Use the available camera views to assess whether temporary conditions (e.g., unusual lane markings, pedestrians, unexpected road work) could be confusing the autonomous system. 3) Communicate with the rider to let them know the vehicle is assessing the situation and that you are reviewing options. 4) If remote guidance is available, follow the standard remote guidance workflow to suggest a safe path if permitted by policy. 5) If remote guidance cannot resolve the situation within a defined time threshold, arrange an alternative ride for the rider and instruct the vehicle to pull over to a safe location if possible. 6) Flag the incident for follow-up with the autonomy and mapping teams, including logs and a brief description of the environment.

section_title: Rider reports leaving an item in the vehicle
section_text: When a rider reports they left an item in a vehicle: 1) Verify the rider's identity using standard verification questions (e.g., recent trip details). 2) Ask the rider to describe the item and approximate time of the trip. 3) Check the internal tools to locate the vehicle and see if the item has already been reported by staff or found in a previous sweep. 4) If the item may still be in the vehicle, create a 'lost item' ticket with trip ID, item description, and rider contact information. 5) Follow the internal process to notify the appropriate depot or operations team to inspect the vehicle. 6) Communicate expected follow-up time to the rider and provide a reference number. 7) Update the ticket when the item is found or confirmed missing and inform the rider of the outcome.

section_title: Item found in vehicle without rider report
section_text: When an item is found in a vehicle by staff or during a routine sweep and no rider has reported it yet: 1) Record the item in the lost and found system with time, vehicle ID, depot, and a clear description of the item. 2) Store the item in the designated secure area at the depot with a matching label or barcode. 3) Periodically match newly reported lost items against the recorded found items by description and date. 4) When a match is likely, verify with the rider using identifying details (e.g., color, brand, unique features) before releasing the item. 5) Document handoff details in the lost and found system.

section_title: Rider reports feeling unsafe
section_text: If a rider reports feeling unsafe (not strictly a medical emergency): 1) Ask the rider to clarify whether the concern is about the vehicle behaviour, another person, or the environment outside the vehicle. 2) If the concern is about vehicle behaviour, review the vehicle status and logs; if needed, request the vehicle to pull over at a safe location. 3) If the concern is due to another person in the vehicle, follow the interpersonal conflict or harassment SOP if applicable. 4) If the concern is about the surrounding environment (e.g., location), consider offering to end the trip at a nearby safer location if policy allows and arrange onward transport if appropriate. 5) Document the concern and any actions taken. If there is any indication of imminent harm, treat the situation as an emergency and follow the emergency SOP. Escalate if: The rider mentions weapons, violence, or threats of any kind.

section_title: Operating during heavy rain or flooding
section_text: When heavy rain or flood warnings are active in the operating area: 1) Monitor weather alerts from the National Weather Service and internal ops dashboard. 2) If roads in the service area are flooded or visibility is critically low, proactively suspend rides in affected zones. 3) For vehicles currently en route, reroute them away from known flood-prone intersections and low-lying underpasses. 4) If a vehicle encounters standing water deeper than 4 inches, instruct it to stop safely and do not attempt to cross. 5) Communicate with riders that weather conditions may cause delays and offer to cancel trips with priority rebooking. 6) Once conditions improve, gradually resume service starting with major corridors and verify road conditions before expanding to secondary streets. 7) Document any weather-related vehicle incidents for the engineering and safety teams.

section_title: Operating during extreme heat (over 110°F)
section_text: When temperatures exceed 110°F (43°C) in the service area: 1) Ensure all vehicles have functioning HVAC before dispatch; any vehicle with reported AC issues should be taken out of service immediately. 2) Monitor cabin temperature sensors remotely; if interior temperature exceeds 85°F with riders on board, alert the rider and offer to end the trip. 3) Reduce maximum rider wait time targets — riders should not wait outdoors longer than 3 minutes in extreme heat. 4) If a rider reports heat-related distress (dizziness, nausea, fainting), treat as a medical emergency per the Medical Emergency SOP. 5) Coordinate with depot teams to ensure vehicles are stored in shaded or covered areas when idle. 6) Increase hydration and break reminders for ground operations staff. Escalate if: A rider loses consciousness due to heat exposure.

section_title: Assisting a rider who uses a wheelchair or mobility aid
section_text: When a rider indicates they use a wheelchair or mobility aid: 1) Confirm the type of mobility aid (manual wheelchair, power wheelchair, walker, crutches) to ensure the dispatched vehicle can accommodate it. 2) Dispatch only wheelchair-accessible vehicles with functioning ramps or lowered entry. 3) If no accessible vehicle is available within the standard wait time, communicate the expected wait time honestly and offer alternatives if available. 4) Once the vehicle arrives, ensure the ramp deploys correctly; if it malfunctions, log the issue and dispatch a replacement immediately. 5) Allow extra boarding and alighting time — do not rush the rider. 6) After the trip, ask the rider if the experience was comfortable and log any feedback for the fleet accessibility review.

section_title: Assisting a visually impaired rider
section_text: When a rider indicates they are visually impaired: 1) Provide clear verbal descriptions of the vehicle (color, nearby landmarks) to help them locate it. 2) If the rider has a guide dog, confirm the vehicle allows service animals (all Zoox vehicles do — this is non-negotiable). 3) Use audio cues through the vehicle's external speaker to help the rider approach: 'Your Zoox vehicle is directly ahead, the door is opening on your left side.' 4) Confirm verbally once they are seated and the door is closed. 5) Provide audio-based trip updates rather than relying on screen displays. 6) At the destination, describe the surroundings briefly before the rider exits: direction of the nearest sidewalk, any obstacles, building entrance location.

section_title: Navigating steep hills in San Francisco
section_text: San Francisco has grades exceeding 25% on some streets. Special procedures: 1) The autonomy system is configured to avoid streets with grades over 31.5% (like Bradford Street). If a rider requests a destination on an extremely steep street, the vehicle will stop at the nearest safe flat intersection. 2) Inform the rider: 'Due to the steep grade, the vehicle will drop you at [nearest safe location], which is approximately [X] feet from your destination.' 3) For vehicles stopped on hills, ensure the parking brake is always engaged before opening doors. 4) In wet conditions, reduce operating speed on streets with grades over 15% and avoid routes via Lombard Street's crooked section. 5) Report any hill-related autonomy issues to the SF mapping team with GPS coordinates and grade estimate.

section_title: Operating in San Francisco fog conditions
section_text: San Francisco frequently experiences dense fog, especially in the Sunset and Richmond districts and near the Golden Gate Bridge: 1) When visibility drops below 500 feet as reported by onboard sensors, the vehicle automatically reduces maximum speed to 25 mph. 2) If visibility drops below 200 feet, consider suspending service in the affected zone and notify riders of delays. 3) Use enhanced sensor fusion mode (LiDAR priority over camera) which is optimized for fog conditions. 4) Common fog-affected routes include Great Highway, Sunset Boulevard, and 19th Avenue — monitor these corridors closely during morning hours (6-10 AM). 5) Riders may report the vehicle is 'driving too slowly' — explain that the vehicle adjusts speed for safety in reduced visibility.

section_title: Managing service during San Francisco events (Giants games, Outside Lands, etc.)
section_text: During major San Francisco events: 1) Check the SF events calendar daily and pre-configure geo-fences for known road closures at least 24 hours in advance. 2) For Giants games at Oracle Park: expect heavy pedestrian traffic on King Street and 3rd Street 90 minutes before and 30 minutes after games. Temporarily suspend pickups/dropoffs within 2 blocks of the stadium during these windows. 3) For Outside Lands (Golden Gate Park): the western half of the park is closed to vehicles; reroute around via Fulton Street and Lincoln Way. 4) For Pride Parade (Market Street): the entire Market Street corridor is closed; use Mission Street or Howard Street as alternatives. 5) Communicate event-related delays to riders proactively with estimated additional travel time.

section_title: Operating on and near the Las Vegas Strip
section_text: The Las Vegas Strip (Las Vegas Blvd between Russell Rd and Sahara Ave) has unique operational challenges: 1) Expect extremely heavy pedestrian jaywalking, especially between 10 PM and 3 AM on weekends. The vehicle's pedestrian detection must be at maximum sensitivity in this zone. 2) Pickups and dropoffs should be directed to designated ride-share zones at each casino, not the main Strip sidewalk. 3) Large tourist groups frequently step into the roadway for photos — the vehicle may stop frequently. Reassure riders this is normal. 4) During major events (CES, Formula 1, UFC fights), expect road closures and 2-3x normal traffic. Pre-load alternative routing. 5) Limousines and party buses make unpredictable stops — maintain extra following distance on the Strip. 6) After 2 AM, watch for intoxicated pedestrians who may approach or lean on the vehicle.

section_title: Handling frequent construction zones in Las Vegas
section_text: Las Vegas has continuous construction due to rapid development: 1) The mapping team updates construction zone data weekly, but new construction may appear between updates. 2) If a vehicle encounters an unexpected construction zone with altered lane markings or temporary signals, attempt remote guidance first. 3) Common construction hotspots include: the I-15/Tropicana interchange, the Las Vegas Convention Center area, and Summerlin Parkway. 4) Construction workers may attempt to manually direct traffic — the vehicle cannot interpret hand signals. If a construction worker is directing traffic, stop the vehicle and use remote guidance to proceed safely. 5) Night construction is common in Las Vegas to avoid daytime heat; expect lane closures between 10 PM and 5 AM on major roads. 6) Log all new construction zone encounters for the mapping team with photos if available.

section_title: Operating in Foster City residential neighborhoods
section_text: Foster City is primarily residential with specific considerations: 1) Speed limits are strictly 25 mph on all residential streets — the vehicle must comply even if the road appears empty. 2) Many streets have speed bumps; ensure the vehicle reduces to 15 mph over speed bumps to maintain rider comfort. 3) School zones are active on weekdays 7:30-8:30 AM and 2:30-3:30 PM near Audubon Elementary, Foster City Elementary, and Bowditch Middle School. Speed must reduce to 15 mph during these windows. 4) The lagoon path crossings on East Hillsdale Blvd and Shell Blvd have heavy pedestrian and cyclist traffic on weekends — extra caution required. 5) Parking is tight on residential streets; use designated ride-share pullover spots marked in the mapping data to avoid blocking driveways.

section_title: Operating in downtown Austin and 6th Street area
section_text: Downtown Austin, especially the 6th Street entertainment district, requires special procedures: 1) On Friday and Saturday nights (10 PM - 2 AM), 6th Street between Congress Ave and I-35 is closed to vehicle traffic. Do not route through this area during these hours. 2) The Congress Avenue Bridge area experiences heavy pedestrian traffic at sunset from March through October (bat watching season) — expect sudden pedestrian surges. 3) Scooters and e-bikes are extremely common in downtown Austin and often ride unpredictably in vehicle lanes. Maintain heightened detection sensitivity. 4) During SXSW (typically March), ACL Festival (October), and UT football game days, expect significant road closures and 3-4x normal demand. Pre-configure event routing 48 hours in advance. 5) Austin's growing network of protected bike lanes on Guadalupe, Lavaca, and Rio Grande requires careful lane positioning.

section_title: Handling severe weather in Austin (thunderstorms, ice, tornadoes)
section_text: Austin is prone to severe weather events: 1) Flash flooding: Austin's terrain creates dangerous flash flood conditions. NEVER allow a vehicle to cross a flooded road — Texas law ('Turn Around, Don't Drown') applies. If flood warnings are issued for specific creek crossings (Shoal Creek, Waller Creek, Onion Creek), immediately blacklist those road segments. 2) Ice storms: Austin roads are not treated for ice. If temperatures drop below 32°F with precipitation, suspend all operations until road conditions are verified safe. Austin drivers are inexperienced with ice — expect erratic behavior from other vehicles. 3) Tornado warnings: Immediately stop all vehicles in a safe location away from overpasses and large trees. Notify riders to remain in the vehicle (it's safer than being outside) unless directed to a storm shelter. 4) Hail: If hail larger than 1 inch is forecast, route vehicles to covered parking or depots. Escalate if: Any vehicle sustains hail damage, or a rider is stranded during a tornado warning.

section_title: Handling sensor malfunction during a trip
section_text: If a vehicle reports a sensor malfunction (LiDAR, camera, radar, or ultrasonic) while carrying riders: 1) Check the severity level on the ops dashboard — Level 1 (degraded but safe) vs Level 2 (critical, affects safe operation). 2) For Level 1: The vehicle can continue to a safe pullover point at reduced speed. Notify the rider of a brief stop and arrange a replacement vehicle. 3) For Level 2: The vehicle will automatically execute an emergency stop. Immediately communicate with the rider: 'The vehicle has detected an issue and has stopped safely. A replacement ride is being arranged.' 4) Do NOT instruct the vehicle to continue driving with Level 2 sensor failures. 5) Notify the maintenance team with vehicle ID, sensor type, error code, and GPS location. 6) Flag the vehicle as 'out of service' until maintenance clears it. Escalate if: Multiple vehicles report the same sensor type failing in a short time period (possible systematic issue).

section_title: Vehicle reports flat tire or tire pressure warning
section_text: If a vehicle reports a tire pressure warning or suspected flat tire: 1) Check the tire pressure data on the ops dashboard. If any tire is below 20 PSI, the vehicle should not continue. 2) If the vehicle is carrying riders, instruct it to pull over at the nearest safe, flat location (avoid hills). 3) Inform the rider: 'The vehicle has detected a tire issue and is stopping safely. We are arranging an alternative ride for you.' 4) Dispatch a replacement vehicle immediately. 5) Contact the nearest depot for a recovery tow — the vehicle cannot drive on a flat tire. 6) If the tire pressure is between 20-28 PSI (low but not flat), the vehicle may drive at reduced speed (under 25 mph) to the nearest depot ONLY if no riders are on board. 7) Log the incident with tire position, pressure reading, and road conditions.

section_title: Rider complains about ride quality (jerky, slow, uncomfortable)
section_text: When a rider complains about ride quality: 1) Acknowledge the feedback empathetically: 'I understand that wasn't a comfortable experience. Thank you for letting us know.' 2) Ask specific questions: Was the vehicle braking too hard? Making unnecessary stops? Taking a strange route? Going too slowly? 3) Check the trip log for any anomalies (hard braking events, unusual route deviations, speed profile). 4) If the ride is still in progress and the issue is ongoing, offer to end the trip early at a safe location and arrange a replacement. 5) Log the complaint with: trip ID, rider description of the issue, any correlated vehicle data. 6) Offer a ride credit if the complaint is verified by trip data (e.g., more than 3 hard braking events, route more than 30% longer than expected). 7) Forward repeated ride quality complaints about the same route or area to the autonomy tuning team.

section_title: Rider disputes a charge or billing issue
section_text: When a rider contacts support about a billing issue: 1) Verify the rider's identity and pull up the trip in question. 2) Compare the charged amount against the trip details (distance, time, surge pricing if applicable). 3) Common billing disputes: a) 'I was charged for a ride I didn't take' — check if the trip shows GPS movement and rider presence. If no evidence of a rider, refund immediately. b) 'The fare was higher than the estimate' — check if surge pricing was active, or if the route was significantly longer due to road closures. Explain the discrepancy. c) 'I was charged twice' — check for duplicate transaction IDs. If confirmed duplicate, issue a refund for the duplicate charge. 4) If the dispute is valid, issue a refund or credit within 24 hours. 5) If the dispute is not clearly valid, escalate to the billing team with all evidence. Do not deny a refund in the initial call; always say: 'I'm forwarding this to our billing team for review, and they will follow up within 24 hours.'

section_title: Guiding a first-time Zoox rider
section_text: When supporting a first-time Zoox rider: 1) Be patient and encouraging — their first autonomous ride may feel unfamiliar. 2) Explain the basics: 'The vehicle is fully autonomous. There is no driver. You can sit anywhere — the vehicle has forward-facing seats on both sides.' 3) Explain how to interact: 'The screen in front of you shows the route, ETA, and trip status. You can tap the Help button on the screen at any time to reach us.' 4) Explain safety features: 'The vehicle has multiple sensor systems and is continuously monitoring the road. It will stop if anything unexpected happens.' 5) Explain the door: 'The doors open and close automatically. Please wait until the door fully opens before entering or exiting. Keep hands clear of the door edges.' 6) At the end of the ride: 'We hope you enjoyed your first Zoox ride! If you have any feedback, you can rate the ride in the app.'

section_title: Vehicle involved in a minor collision (no injuries)
section_text: If a Zoox vehicle is involved in a minor collision with no apparent injuries: 1) Immediately check rider status via the intercom: 'Are you okay? Is anyone hurt?' 2) If riders confirm they are fine, instruct the vehicle to remain stopped. Do not move it from the accident scene. 3) Call local police to file an accident report — this is required for all collisions regardless of severity. 4) Collect information from the other party if possible: license plate, insurance info, driver contact. Use the vehicle's cameras to capture the scene. 5) Arrange an alternative ride for the riders immediately. 6) Notify the safety lead and legal team. 7) Do NOT admit fault or apologize on behalf of Zoox — say: 'Our team will review all sensor data and cooperate fully with any investigation.' 8) Save all vehicle logs, camera footage, and sensor data from 5 minutes before to 5 minutes after the incident. Escalate immediately to: Safety Lead AND Legal Team.

section_title: Vehicle involved in a major collision with injuries
section_text: If a Zoox vehicle is involved in a collision where injuries are reported or suspected: 1) Call 911 IMMEDIATELY. Provide: exact GPS location, number of people involved, description of injuries if known. 2) Stay on the line with riders if they can communicate. Provide reassurance but do NOT give medical advice. 3) Do NOT move the vehicle under any circumstances. 4) Notify the Safety Lead, VP of Operations, and Legal immediately — this is a Priority 1 escalation. 5) If media or bystanders approach, do not provide any statements. Say: 'Our priority is the safety of everyone involved. Our team is cooperating with emergency services.' 6) Preserve all vehicle data — lock the logs and camera footage immediately. 7) Deploy a ground response team to the scene if available. 8) Begin the Serious Incident Protocol: document timeline of events, all communications, and all decisions made. Escalate IMMEDIATELY to: Safety Lead, VP of Operations, Legal, and PR team.
